description: The currently visible image is an irrelevant software 'Check for updates' icon placeholder, which does not contain any valid content related to the research on Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel manufactured by selective laser melting. No microstructure, property testing data, synthesis process, characterization result or other material science related information for the target research can be identified from this provided image, so no valid figure-level information matching the target paper content can be extracted from the current visual content.

This figure is a schematic diagram illustrating the selective laser melting (SLM) fabrication process of Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel. The upper part shows the overall SLM setup, including a powder feed container, overflow container, and laser source irradiating on the pre-laid powder bed. The lower left inset demonstrates the scanning strategy: the scanning path of adjacent layers (Layer n and Layer n+1) is rotated by 67° to reduce crystallographic texture by avoiding laser vector coincidence between consecutive layers. The lower right inset presents a magnified view of laser-powder interaction, showing the moving laser forming a melt pool to melt Fe30Mn9AlC3Ni0.6Cr powder and fabricate parts on the substrate. This schematic explains the SLM preparation principle for the studied low-density steel, and confirms the 67° interlayer rotation scanning strategy adopted to minimize texture, supporting the subsequent process parameter optimization research.

This figure presents the characterization results of Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel powder fabricated via plasma rotating electrode process (PREP). Subfigures (a) and (b) are SEM images at different magnifications with scales of 400 μm and 10 μm respectively, showing the powder has high sphericity, no satellites or irregular particles, and uniform particle size distribution. Subfigures (c) to (f) are EDS elemental mapping images of Fe, Mn, Al and Ni respectively, demonstrating that all four alloying elements are uniformly distributed within the powder particles. Subfigure (g) is the cross-sectional SEM image of a single powder particle with a scale of 2 μm, confirming the powder has a solid interior without obvious pores. These results verify that the PREP-prepared powder possesses excellent morphology, compositional homogeneity and compactness, which is suitable as feedstock for selective laser melting (SLM).

This figure displays the particle size distribution of Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel powder manufactured by plasma rotating electrode process (PREP). The blue histogram shows the volume percentage of powder particles for each particle size interval, and the red curve represents the cumulative volume percentage of particles. As extracted from the plot and supporting text, the particle sizes of the prepared powder are mainly concentrated in the 15–53 μm range, with a median particle size (D50) of 35.69 μm. The as-prepared powder shows good flowability with a Hall flow rate of 14 s/50 g, proving it is suitable as feedstock for the selective laser melting (SLM) process.

This figure displays the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel powder manufactured by plasma rotating electrode process (PREP). The x-axis shows 2θ in degrees ranging from 30° to 100°, and the y-axis represents diffraction intensity in arbitrary units (a.u.). Two crystalline phases are identified from the diffraction peaks: the dominant γ-Fe(M) (austenite) phase, marked by black dots, with indexed crystal planes of (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222); and the minor α-Fe(M) (ferrite) phase, marked by red downward triangles, with indexed crystal planes of (110) and (200). The pattern verifies that γ-Fe(M) is the primary phase in the PREP-produced powder, with small amount of α-Fe(M) formed due to relatively slow cooling during PREP, which allows small carbon dissolution in α-Fe to promote ferrite phase formation, supporting subsequent phase evolution analysis for selective laser melting of this alloy.

This figure shows X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel samples manufactured by selective laser melting (SLM) under five different laser powers, corresponding to samples P-70 (70 W), P-90 (90 W), P-110 (110 W), P-130 (130 W), and P-150 (150 W). The X-axis is 2θ ranging from 30° to 100°, and the Y-axis is diffraction intensity in arbitrary units (a.u.). All detected diffraction peaks are indexed to the face-centered cubic γ-Fe(M) austenite phase, corresponding to (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222) crystal planes, with no α-Fe(M) phase peaks observed. The full width at half maximum of the (111) peak decreases sequentially from 0.289 (P-70) to 0.237 (P-150), indicating the average grain size of SLM-fabricated samples increases with rising laser power, as higher energy input leads to slower cooling rate and longer grain growth duration. This result confirms single austenite phase is obtained for all tested SLM parameters, which benefits the strength and toughness of the low-density steel.

This group of optical micrographs shows the transverse cross-sections of Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel samples manufactured by selective laser melting (SLM) under different laser powers. Subfigure (a) corresponds to sample P-70 (70 W laser power) with a porosity of 4.75%, containing a large number of irregular pores; (b) is P-90 (90 W) with porosity 0.25%; (c) is P-110 (110 W) with the lowest porosity of 0.13% and almost no observable defects; (d) is P-130 (130 W) with porosity 0.28%; (e) is P-150 (150 W) with porosity 0.35%, where cracks are also observed. All subfigures have a scale bar of 500 μm. The porosity of the SLM samples first decreases and then increases as laser power rises from 70 W to 150 W, indicating that 110 W is the optimal laser power to achieve the highest densification for this alloy during SLM processing, which corresponds to the best combination of mechanical properties.

This figure presents inverse pole figures (IPFs) from electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) characterization of selective laser melted (SLM) Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density austenitic steel. Subfigures (a) to (e) show transverse (x-y plane) cross-sections of samples fabricated at laser powers of 70 W (P-70), 90 W (P-90), 110 W (P-110), 130 W (P-130), and 150 W (P-150), respectively, while subfigure (f) shows the longitudinal (x-z plane) cross-section of P-110, with a 50 μm scale bar for all subfigures. Colors represent different crystallographic orientations, referenced to the provided austenite IPF triangle for <001>, <101>, and <111> directions. Transverse sections of all samples feature fine equiaxed grains with random orientations, and average grain size increases with increasing laser power, which is attributed to the 67° rotation of the scanning path between each SLM layer. The longitudinal section of P-110 is dominated by columnar grains, with a small fraction of equiaxed grains at the melt pool bottom, caused by the large temperature gradient and vertical heat flow along the deposition direction during solidification. These observations explain the microstructural evolution with SLM parameters and support that P-110 achieves optimal mechanical performance.

This set of histograms presents grain size distributions of selective laser melted (SLM) Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel. Subfigures (a) to (e) show transverse cross-sectional grain size distributions of samples prepared at laser powers of 70 W (P-70), 90 W (P-90), 110 W (P-110), 130 W (P-130), and 150 W (P-150), respectively, while subfigure (f) displays the longitudinal grain size distribution of the P-110 sample. The measured average grain sizes are 8.1 μm for P-70, 10.6 μm for P-90, 11.0 μm for P-110 transverse section, 13.9 μm for P-130, 14.3 μm for P-150, and 14.9 μm for P-110 longitudinal section. A clear trend is observed: as laser power increases from 70 W to 150 W, both the average transverse grain size and the fraction of large grains continuously increase. The larger longitudinal grain size of P-110 is attributed to faster cooling rate in the horizontal direction than the vertical direction during SLM solidification. These grain size variation trends, combined with porosity changes, explain the first increasing then decreasing mechanical properties of the alloys with rising laser power.

This set of figures presents TEM characterization results of the P-110 sample, which is Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel manufactured by selective laser melting (SLM) at 110 W laser power. (a) Bright-field TEM image shows dislocation tangles inside the austenite matrix, induced by thermal expansion of phases during SLM processing. (b) Selected area electron diffraction pattern verifies coexistence of [011]-oriented γ austenite and κ carbide, sharing the same [011] zone axis. (c) Dark-field TEM image confirms uniformly distributed nanoscale κ carbide precipitates in the austenite matrix. (d) Bright-field TEM image displays high-density dislocation regions. (e) High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image measures interplanar spacings of austenite (200) and (1-11) planes as 0.262 nm and 0.223 nm, respectively, with [011] zone axis. (f) Fast Fourier transform pattern of HRTEM confirms the [011] austenite zone axis, no ferrite diffraction spots are observed, proving fast cooling during SLM suppresses ferrite formation. These microstructure features contribute to the excellent mechanical properties of the P-110 sample.

This set of figures displays 2D height-mapped topographies, 3D rendered surface morphologies, and arithmetic mean surface roughness (Sa) of Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel samples manufactured by selective laser melting (SLM) under varied laser powers. Subfigure (a) corresponds to sample P-70 (70 W laser power) with Sa=20.71 μm, (b) to P-90 (90 W) with Sa=9.19 μm, (c) to P-110 (110 W) with Sa=5.29 μm, (d) to P-130 (130 W) with Sa=4.65 μm, and (e) to P-150 (150 W) with Sa=4.19 μm. The surface roughness value decreases continuously with increasing laser power, showing that higher laser power contributes to smoother surfaces of SLM printed alloys, which is favorable for the fabrication of high-precision components.

This group of SEM images displays surface morphologies of selective laser melted (SLM) Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel prepared under different laser powers: (a) 70 W, (b) 90 W, (c) 110 W, (d) 130 W, (e) 150 W, with a uniform scale bar of 200 μm. The sample fabricated at 70 W exhibits severe lack of fusion and hole defects. As laser power increases to 90 W, lack of fusion defects are significantly reduced, and surface quality is further optimized at 110 W with only negligible balling defects. When laser power rises to 130 W and 150 W, balling defects become more prominent due to localized heat accumulation. This figure confirms that moderate laser power around 110 W delivers the best SLM surface quality for the alloy, while insufficient laser power leads to poor fusion defects, and excessive laser power induces aggravated balling defects, consistent with the changing trend of the alloy's mechanical performance.

This figure presents the engineering tensile stress-strain curves of Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steels. The tested samples include selective laser melting (SLM) manufactured specimens prepared at laser powers of 70 W (P-70), 90 W (P-90), 110 W (P-110), 130 W (P-130), 150 W (P-150), and an as-cast counterpart for comparison. The x-axis denotes engineering strain in %, and the y-axis denotes engineering stress in MPa. All SLM-fabricated samples exhibit higher tensile strength and elongation than the as-cast sample: the as-cast alloy has an ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of ~879 MPa and elongation of ~20.5%, while the P-110 sample achieves the optimal comprehensive properties with UTS of ~1077 MPa and elongation of ~40.8%. The tensile properties of SLM samples first increase with laser power from 70 W to 110 W, then decrease as laser power further rises to 150 W. This figure demonstrates that SLM with 110 W laser power produces Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni steel with far superior mechanical performance to the as-cast alloy.

This bar chart presents the Vickers hardness (HV) of longitudinal and cross sections of Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel manufactured by selective laser melting (SLM) under different laser powers: 70W (sample P-70), 90W (P-90), 110W (P-110), 130W (P-130), 150W (P-150). The longitudinal section hardness values are 279 HV, 289 HV, 294 HV, 284 HV, 283 HV for P-70 to P-150 respectively, while cross section hardness values are 278 HV, 285 HV, 292 HV, 283 HV, 281 HV for the corresponding samples. Hardness first increases as laser power rises from 70W to 110W, then decreases with further laser power increment, with the P-110 sample exhibiting the maximum hardness, and longitudinal section hardness slightly higher than cross section hardness for all samples. The hardness variation is ascribed to combined effects of grain size and internal defects (pores, cracks, unfused powder particles), verifying that 110W is the optimized laser power for SLM fabrication of this alloy to achieve superior hardness performance.

This group of SEM images presents tensile fracture surfaces of selective laser melted (SLM) Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel fabricated under different laser powers: (a) P-70 (70 W), (b) P-90 (90 W), (c) P-110 (110 W), (d) P-130 (130 W), (e) P-150 (150 W). Identified fracture features include lack of fusion, cracks, ductile dimples, and brittle cleavage planes. The P-70 sample shows abundant unfused spherical powder particles, lack of fusion defects, and obvious cracks, corresponding to poor tensile performance. Fracture surfaces of P-90 and P-110 are dominated by fine dimples, indicative of superior ductility, yield strength and tensile strength, representing the optimal processing window. For P-130 and P-150, cleavage planes, internal cracks, and spheroidization defects appear alongside dimples, leading to deteriorated mechanical properties. This figure verifies that 110 W is the optimal laser power for SLM fabrication of this alloy to obtain the best comprehensive mechanical performance.
